########## sections/intro.tex ##########
     1	\section{Introduction}
     2	\label{sec:intro}
     3	
     4	Fast radio bursts \citep[FRBs;][]{Lorimer2007, Thornton2013} provide compact,
     5	bright backlights through ionized gas on Galactic, circumgalactic,
     6	intergalactic, and host-galaxy scales. Their dispersion measures trace the
     7	integrated electron column---anchoring the cosmological Macquart relation
     8	\citep{Macquart2020}---while pulse broadening and diffractive scintillation
     9	respond to turbulent structure and screen geometry
    10	\citep[e.g.,][]{CordesChatterjee2019}. Individual sightlines have already
    11	demonstrated the reach of these propagation diagnostics: scattering and
    12	scintillation together can localize a screen to circumburst plasma
    13	\citep{Masui2015}, limit the turbulent column of an intervening galaxy halo
    14	\citep{Prochaska2019, Ocker2021}, or implicate the host interstellar medium as
    15	the dominant scattering site \citep{Ocker2022}. At the population level, the
    16	first CHIME/FRB catalog established that scattering times at 600\,MHz span
    17	several orders of magnitude and are in tension with Galactic-only or IGM-only
    18	origins \citep{CHIME2021Catalog, Chawla2022}. Yet jointly interpreting these
    19	observables for any one burst remains degenerate: a given dispersion measure
    20	can be distributed among multiple media, and a given scattering time can be
    21	produced by different screen locations and turbulence spectra.
    22	
    23	Co-detections by CHIME/FRB and DSA-110 help break these degeneracies by
    24	measuring the same burst over a wide frequency baseline. CHIME/FRB samples
    25	low frequencies at 400--800~MHz, where dispersion delays and scattering are
    26	larger, while DSA-110 observes near 1.4~GHz, where the same event is less
    27	broadened. A single-band measurement constrains only the scattering time at
    28	that band's frequency; the $\sim$1\,GHz lever arm of a co-detection constrains
    29	the frequency scaling of arrival-time structure and pulse broadening directly,
    30	which no single dataset provides alone. It also introduces a critical
    31	association step: the two detections must be demonstrated to be the same
    32	astrophysical burst before their dispersion and scattering information can be
    33	combined.
    34	
    35	This paper is organized around that joint interpretation. We first define the
    36	candidate co-detection sample and the data products used for each telescope. We
    37	then describe the association test, the dispersion-budget model, the foreground
    38	census used to identify potential intervening screens, the two-band scattering
    39	model, the scintillation analysis, and the band-limited energy calculation.
    40	The association tests, the foreground census, and the dispersion budget are
    41	reported in full; the scattering, scintillation, turbulence, and energetics
    42	results are developed in their respective sections.
########## sections/observations.tex ##########
     1	\section{Observations and Sample}
     2	\label{sec:obs}
     3	
     4	The working sample is the set of candidate fast-radio-burst co-detections seen
     5	by CHIME/FRB \citep{CHIMEFRB2018} at $0.4$--$0.8$\,GHz and by DSA-110
     6	\citep{Law2024} at $\sim$1.4\,GHz between 2022 February and 2024 February. The
     7	defining asset of the sample is that each candidate event is observed across a
     8	wide frequency baseline, which---as developed in Section~\ref{sec:jointfit}---can
     9	separate scattering time from frequency scaling more strongly than a single-band
    10	fit. The twelve bursts, their TNS designations, sky positions,
    11	detection epochs, and association diagnostics are listed in
    12	Table~\ref{tab:sample}. The timing residuals in that table use the shared
    13	DSA-DM reference convention; the per-telescope DM values and their CHIME--DSA
    14	agreement are provided as a machine-readable provenance table in the archival
    15	data release (Section~\ref{sec:data-availability}).
    16	
    17	\input{sample_table.tex}
    18	
    19	\subsection{Dynamic spectra and reduction}
    20	\label{sec:data}
    21	
    22	For each co-detection we analyze a pair of total-intensity dynamic spectra---one
    23	per facility---derived from the CHIME/FRB and DSA-110 baseband recordings
    24	(24 spectra in total; per-spectrum provenance and array geometry accompany the
    25	archival data release, Section~\ref{sec:data-availability}). Each spectrum is
    26	loaded with its frequency axis
    27	standardized to ascending order and its per-channel bandpass normalized, so the
    28	fitted amplitudes are free of the band-edge rolloff. All fits, quality gates,
    29	and figures in this paper are produced with a common open-source reduction and
    30	fitting pipeline, which we reference throughout for reproducibility. We do not catalog the fine
    31	baseband morphology of each burst here; the only morphological distinction the
    32	analysis depends on---whether a burst resolves into more than one temporal
    33	component---is treated where it matters, in the multi-component fit of
    34	Section~\ref{sec:multicomp}.
    35	
    36	\subsection{Scattering fits}
    37	\label{sec:obs-scatt}
    38	
    39	The Methods section defines the joint two-band model, the $\beta$-based
    40	scattering closure, the temporal multiplicity treatment, and the quality gates
    41	used to decide which per-sightline quantities are citable
    42	(Sections~\ref{sec:jointfit} and~\ref{sec:multicomp}). Scattering-fit results
    43	are deferred pending completion of the scattering analysis
    44	(Section~\ref{sec:results-alpha}).
    45	
    46	% TODO(obs-scattering): Reinsert any joint-fit montage only after the fit
    47	% products shown in the figure have passed the re-validation contract.
    48	
    49	\subsection{Milky Way foreground contributions}
    50	\label{sec:obs-mw}
    51	
    52	To characterize the expected propagation effects contributed by the Milky Way, we query the NE2025 Galactic electron density model \citep{Ocker2026} along each of the twelve co-detection sightlines. The expected Galactic dispersion measure ($\text{DM}_{\text{MW}}$) is integrated to a distance of 30\,kpc. The pulse-broadening timescale ($\tau_{\text{MW}}$) and scintillation bandwidth ($\Delta\nu_{\text{MW}}$) are scaled from the NE2025 output at 1\,GHz to the center of the CHIME/FRB band (600\,MHz) and the DSA-110 band (1.4\,GHz) assuming Kolmogorov scaling ($\tau \propto \nu^{-4.4}$ and $\Delta\nu_d \propto \nu^{4.4}$).
    53	
    54	We flag one methodological asymmetry deliberately. This Galactic prediction
    55	uses a \emph{fixed} Kolmogorov frequency index ($\alpha=4.4$) to scale
    56	$\tau_{\text{MW}}$ between bands, whereas the extragalactic scattering analysis
    57	of Section~\ref{sec:jointfit} co-models the frequency scaling with the pulse
    58	shape through the turbulence index $\beta$ rather than fixing $\alpha$. We keep
    59	the fixed-$\alpha$ Kolmogorov value here as a fiducial for the \emph{Galactic}
    60	term because NE2025 is itself calibrated on a Kolmogorov medium and because the
    61	predicted Galactic broadening is sub-microsecond on every high-latitude
    62	sightline (below), so the choice of Galactic scaling index cannot affect the
    63	extragalactic attribution there. On the low-latitude sightlines, where
    64	$\tau_{\text{MW}}$ is non-negligible, we carry this as an explicit systematic
    65	rather than propagate the fitted $\beta$ posterior through a model (NE2025) that
    66	does not itself expose one.
    67	
    68	As shown in Figure~\ref{fig:ne2025_mw}, the sightlines exhibit strongly
    69	latitude-dependent behavior. High-latitude sightlines ($|b| > 30^\circ$)
    70	present minimal Galactic contamination, with expected Milky Way dispersion
    71	contributions $\text{DM}_{\text{MW}} \lesssim 41$\,pc\,cm$^{-3}$ and
    72	sub-microsecond scattering broadening at CHIME frequencies
    73	($\tau_{\text{MW}} \lesssim 0.1\,\mu$s). Low-latitude sightlines
    74	($|b| < 15^\circ$) carry substantial Galactic contributions, with
    75	$\text{DM}_{\text{MW}}$ reaching $\approx 95$\,pc\,cm$^{-3}$ and predicted
    76	CHIME-band scattering timescales of $\tau_{\text{MW}} \gtrsim 30\,\mu$s,
    77	which can dominate or mask the intrinsic extragalactic scattering.
    78	
    79	The $\text{DM}_{\text{MW}}$ quoted in this section is the NE2025 \emph{disk}
    80	(ISM) column alone. In the dispersion budget of
    81	Section~\ref{sec:dm} (Table~\ref{tab:budget}) we add the
    82	$40\,\mathrm{pc\,cm^{-3}}$ Milky-Way halo prior to this disk term, so the
    83	$\mathrm{DM_{MW}}$ column there ($74$--$137\,\mathrm{pc\,cm^{-3}}$) is larger
    84	than the $\lesssim95\,\mathrm{pc\,cm^{-3}}$ disk-only values here by that fixed
    85	halo offset; the two are consistent once the halo term is accounted for.
    86	
    87	\begin{figure*}
    88	    \centering
    89	    \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figures/ne2025_mw_characterization_nside32.pdf}
    90	    \caption{NE2025 Galactic electron density model predictions. Top row: All-sky Mollweide maps (in Galactic coordinates) showing predicted dispersion measure ($\text{DM}_{\text{MW}}$, left), pulse-broadening timescale ($\tau_{\text{MW}}$ at 1\,GHz, middle), and scintillation bandwidth ($\Delta\nu_{\text{MW}}$ at 1\,GHz, right). Yellow stars indicate the positions of the twelve sightlines. Bottom row: Individual sightline values plotted against absolute Galactic latitude $|b|$ for dispersion (left), scaled scattering timescale (middle; CHIME at 600\,MHz in orange, DSA-110 at 1.4\,GHz in blue), and scaled scintillation bandwidth (right). Scatter plots show values calculated assuming Kolmogorov scaling ($\alpha = 4.4$) \citep{Ocker2026}.}
    91	    \label{fig:ne2025_mw}
    92	\end{figure*}
    93	
    94	\subsection{Foreground-galaxy search}
    95	\label{sec:obs-fg}
    96	
    97	Before the intervening dispersion and scattering terms can be computed, each
    98	sightline must be searched for foreground galaxies and clusters, and every
    99	candidate assigned a redshift and a foreground/background verdict. We run the
   100	search in two stages---discovery followed by external-catalog
   101	validation---and record the result in Table~\ref{tab:foreground}.
   102	
   103	The discovery stage searches a wide cone around each localized burst. At the
   104	sample's high declination ($+70^\circ$ to $+74^\circ$) only all-sky or northern
   105	catalogs provide coverage. For galaxy-scale halos we query the DESI Legacy
   106	Imaging Surveys photometric-redshift catalog \citep[the deep-learning estimator
   107	of][]{Zhou2021}, GLADE+, and SDSS~DR12, supplemented by the
   108	WISE--PanSTARRS--STRM photometric classifier \citep{Beck2021} for sources
   109	without a Legacy match; for clusters we query the DESI Legacy/WISE cluster
   110	catalog of \citet{WenHan2024}---which supplies $M_{500}$, $R_{500}$, and,
   111	through its DESI members, a spectroscopic redshift---together with the all-sky
   112	Planck Sunyaev--Zel'dovich (PSZ2) and X-ray (MCXC, MCXC-II) catalogs; at the
   113	sample's declination every matched cluster comes from the optical catalog.
   114	The search apertures set the census completeness and are fixed in physical
   115	units. Galaxy cones extend to a fixed proper impact parameter at the
   116	circumgalactic scale, and the recovered foreground galaxies span
   117	$b\approx60$--$280\,\mathrm{kpc}$; cluster cones are far wider
   118	($\sim\!5\,\mathrm{Mpc}$), and a cluster is retained when the sightline passes
   119	within twice its virial radius ($\lesssim2\,R_{200}\approx3\,R_{500}$), so a
   120	sightline that grazes a cluster outskirt is still recovered. Only crossings
   121	within $R_{500}$ enter the dispersion budget (Section~\ref{sec:foreground}); the
   122	wider retention aperture exists so the excluded near-misses can be shown
   123	explicitly (Appendix~\ref{app:clusters}).
   124	
   125	Validation cross-matches every candidate, by coordinate, against three
   126	independent redshift sources: the DESI Legacy Survey DR9 photometric redshifts
   127	(the \citealt{Zhou2021} random-forest estimator, which supplies a per-object
   128	redshift error and a morphological type), DESI~DR1 spectroscopic redshifts, and
   129	the NASA/IPAC Extragalactic Database (NED). Matches use a $5''$ radius for
   130	galaxies and a $90''$ radius for cluster centroids. The adopted redshift follows
   131	a fixed priority---DESI spectroscopic, then Legacy spectroscopic, then Legacy
   132	photometric (carrying its $1\sigma$ error), then NED---and a DESI spectrum
   133	classified as a star is rejected outright, so a foreground stellar velocity can
   134	never be adopted as a system redshift. A candidate is \emph{confirmed} when its
   135	redshift (or photometric redshift plus $1\sigma$) lies below the host,
   136	\emph{refuted} when it lies above, and \emph{inconclusive} when it falls within
   137	$1\sigma$ of the host, when the host redshift is a placeholder, or when no
   138	trustworthy redshift or galaxy classification exists (including the
   139	PanSTARRS-STRM ``unsure'' class).
   140	
   141	The census evaluated 35 candidate systems along the twelve sightlines.
   142	Table~\ref{tab:foreground} lists the 28 that remain relevant to the budget:
   143	15 confirmed foreground (14 galaxy halos and the single cluster whose
   144	sightline passes within $R_{500}$) and 13 inconclusive---candidates the
   145	available redshifts could not place on either side of the host, which
   146	therefore bound the systematic uncertainty on each sightline's intervening
   147	column. The remaining 7 candidates were refuted as background by the
   148	validation stage and are omitted from the table, as are candidates rejected
   149	outright as stars. A further 14 spectroscopically confirmed
   150	foreground clusters lie along these sightlines at impact parameters beyond
   151	$R_{500}$; dynamically irrelevant to the dispersion budget, they are likewise
   152	omitted---the \citet{WenHan2024} catalog reaches group scale
   153	($M_{500}>0.47\times10^{14}\,M_\odot$), so several are low-mass systems. Because
   154	only wide-field catalogs reach this declination, a sightline with no confirmed
   155	system is unconstrained by survey depth rather than demonstrably foreground-free;
   156	the budget of Section~\ref{sec:foreground} treats such sightlines accordingly.
   157	
   158	Of the 15 confirmed foreground systems, nine carry a DESI or NED spectroscopic
   159	(or the single cluster's DESI-member) redshift; the remaining six rest on a
   160	DESI Legacy DR9 photometric redshift alone. Our confirmation criterion
   161	($z_{\rm phot}+1\sigma < z_{\rm host}$) admits, per object, the $\sim16\%$
   162	Gaussian probability that the true redshift exceeds $z_{\rm phot}+1\sigma$, plus
   163	the few-percent catastrophic-outlier rate characteristic of Legacy DR9
   164	photometric redshifts. Across the six photometric confirmations this amounts to
   165	of order one expected misclassification of a background galaxy as foreground.
   166	Each of these six lies in the outer circumgalactic medium
   167	($b\approx105$--$280\,\mathrm{kpc}$) and contributes only a small dispersion
   168	column, so a misclassification would remove at most that object's column from
   169	$\mathrm{DM_{int}}$; we therefore carry the summed photometric-confirmation
   170	column as a one-sided systematic on $\mathrm{DM_{int}}$ for the affected
   171	sightlines (Section~\ref{sec:foreground}) rather than treating those
   172	confirmations as exact.
   173	Figure~\ref{fig:sightline_halo_grid} shows the foreground-halo population used
   174	for this census as a function of redshift and signed impact parameter.
   175	
   176	\begin{figure*}[tp]
   177	    \centering
   178	    \includegraphics[width=\textwidth,height=0.9\textheight,keepaspectratio]{figures/sightline_halo_grid.pdf}
   179	    \caption{Foreground-halo census along the co-detection sightlines. Each panel
   180	    is one FRB sightline (labeled with its host redshift), showing every
   181	    foreground halo ($z<z_{\rm host}$) recovered from the DESI Legacy/WISE and
   182	    GLADE+ catalogs as a function of redshift and signed projected impact
   183	    parameter. Each halo is a soft disk whose radius is its projected virial
   184	    radius ($R_{200}$) and whose color encodes halo mass
   185	    $\log_{10}(M_{\rm halo}/M_\odot)$; the shaded horizontal band marks the inner
   186	    $\pm300$\,kpc impact corridor and the black inclined-disk galaxy symbol marks the
   187	    FRB host at $(z_{\rm host},0)$. The single halo the sightline pierces within $R_{500}$ at
   188	    cluster mass ($M_{200}>10^{14}\,M_\odot$)---the intervening system that
   189	    enters the dispersion budget of Section~\ref{sec:foreground}---is ringed in
   190	    black; it lies in the dense field toward FRB~20230307A, the one such crossing
   191	    in the sample. Nine of the twelve sightlines are shown, ordered by host
   192	    redshift; the three without a spectroscopic host redshift
   193	    (FRB~20230325A, FRB~20240122A, FRB~20230814B) are omitted, and sightlines
   194	    with no recovered foreground halo (e.g. the low-redshift FRB~20220207C) are
   195	    shown empty. The key panel gives the $R_{200}$ size scale and marker legend.}
   196	    \label{fig:sightline_halo_grid}
   197	\end{figure*}
   198	
   199	\input{foreground_table}
########## sections/methods.tex ##########
     1	\section{Methods}
     2	\label{sec:methods}
     3	
     4	This section defines how the co-detections are associated, how the dispersion
     5	and scattering budgets are modeled, and how the fitted quantities used in
     6	Section~\ref{sec:results} are validated.
     7	
     8	\input{sections/toa.tex}
     9	\input{sections/budget.tex}
    10	
    11	\subsection{Band-restricted burst energies}
    12	\label{sec:methods-energies}
    13	
    14	For each sightline with a spectroscopic host redshift and a trusted per-band
    15	amplitude fit, we estimate the isotropic-equivalent burst energy directly
    16	from the joint CHIME--DSA fit, without extrapolating either band's spectrum
    17	beyond where it is constrained.
    18	The fit returns a per-band spectral amplitude and index,
    19	$F_X(\nu) = c_{0,X}\,(\nu/\nu_{\mathrm{ref},X})^{\gamma_X}$, which we place on
    20	an absolute scale with each telescope's per-channel radiometer conversion
    21	$\sigma_{S,X}(\nu) = \mathrm{SEFD}_X(\nu)\,/\,[\sqrt{n_{\mathrm{pol}}\,\Delta\nu\,\Delta t}\,G_X(\nu)]$,
    22	taking the system-equivalent flux density and primary-beam gain $G_X$ from the
    23	DSA-110 \citep{Law2024} and CHIME/FRB \citep{CHIMEFRB2018,Michilli2021} flux
    24	scales. We integrate over each instrument's observing band---CHIME over
    25	$0.400$--$0.800\,\mathrm{GHz}$ and DSA over $1.311$--$1.499\,\mathrm{GHz}$,
    26	\begin{equation}
    27	E_{\mathrm{iso}} = \frac{4\pi D_L^2(z)}{1+z}\left[
    28	  \int_{\nu_1^{C}}^{\nu_2^{C}} s_C\,F_{\mathrm{CHIME}}(\nu)\,d\nu
    29	  + \int_{\nu_1^{D}}^{\nu_2^{D}} s_D\,F_{\mathrm{DSA}}(\nu)\,d\nu
    30	\right],
    31	\label{eq:eiso}
    32	\end{equation}
    33	with $D_L(z)$ from the fiducial \textit{Planck}\,2018 cosmology
    34	\citep{Planck2018}, the $(1+z)$ bandwidth k-correction applied, and
    35	$1\,\mathrm{Jy\,ms\,Hz} = 10^{-29}\,\mathrm{J\,m^{-2}}$. This band-restricted
    36	integral constrains the energy released within the observed spectral envelope:
    37	it avoids the large, model-dependent extrapolation that a single power-law fit
    38	across the full $0.4$--$1.5\,\mathrm{GHz}$ span would require, at the cost of not
    39	counting flux in the unobserved $0.8$--$1.3\,\mathrm{GHz}$ gap.
    40	
    41	Two properties of this definition must be stated prominently. First, the
    42	observed bands map to \emph{per-burst} rest-frame intervals: CHIME's
    43	$0.400$--$0.800\,\mathrm{GHz}$ samples the rest-frame band
    44	$[0.400(1+z),\,0.800(1+z)]\,\mathrm{GHz}$ and DSA's
    45	$1.311$--$1.499\,\mathrm{GHz}$ samples $[1.311(1+z),\,1.499(1+z)]\,\mathrm{GHz}$,
    46	so the rest-frame window shifts from burst to burst. These band-restricted
    47	energies are therefore \emph{not} comparable to literature energies computed
    48	over a fixed rest-frame band, and cannot be placed on a standard FRB luminosity
    49	function without a spectral-model extrapolation that we deliberately avoid here;
    50	we report them as observed-band quantities and, where a comparison is needed,
    51	provide a fixed rest-frame-band variant alongside. Second, the single $(1+z)$ in
    52	Eq.~\ref{eq:eiso} is the bandwidth (time-dilation) k-correction, which is common
    53	to both bands; the differing per-band spectral indices $\gamma_C,\gamma_D$ enter
    54	only the in-band flux integrals $\int F_X(\nu)\,d\nu$, not the k-correction, so
    55	the two-band sum is internally consistent despite the single $(1+z)$ factor.
    56	Energy rows are deferred until the spectral
    57	amplitudes, calibration path, host-redshift provenance, and inclusion rule are
    58	ready to be described and cited in the manuscript.
########## sections/toa.tex ##########
     1	\subsection{Time-of-Arrival Crossmatching}
     2	\label{sec:toa}
     3	
     4	Before attributing the dispersion and scattering budgets we verify that the
     5	twelve events are genuine co-detections---the same burst seen by both
     6	facilities, with arrival times consistent under the cold-plasma dispersion law
     7	and the geometric light-travel delay between the sites. The wide baseline
     8	between CHIME/FRB (400--800\,MHz) and DSA-110 ($\sim$1.4\,GHz) makes the
     9	co-detections a test of the $1/\nu^2$ dispersion scaling and of the relative
    10	timing alignment of the two facilities \citep{Lorimer2007, Thornton2013}.
    11	
    12	\subsubsection{Delay model}
    13	
    14	We translate the arrival times at the two central frequencies to a common
    15	reference frequency $\nu_{\mathrm{ref}}=400$\,MHz. The cold-plasma dispersion
    16	delay is
    17	\begin{equation}
    18	\Delta t_p = K_{\mathrm{DM}}\,\mathrm{DM}
    19	\left(\frac{1}{\nu_{\mathrm{ref}}^2}-\frac{1}{\nu_{\mathrm{obs}}^2}\right),
    20	\label{eq:dmdelay}
    21	\end{equation}
    22	with $K_{\mathrm{DM}} = 4.148808\times10^{3}\,\mathrm{MHz^2\,pc^{-1}\,cm^3\,s}$.
    23	The geometric light-travel difference between the observatories is
    24	\begin{equation}
    25	\tau_{\mathrm{geo}} = \frac{(\mathbf{p}_2-\mathbf{p}_1)\cdot\hat{\mathbf{s}}}{c},
    26	\label{eq:geodelay}
    27	\end{equation}
    28	where $\mathbf{p}_1$ and $\mathbf{p}_2$ are the GCRS position vectors of CHIME
    29	and DSA-110 and $\hat{\mathbf{s}}$ is the unit vector toward the source. The
    30	timing residual is the difference between the frequency-corrected inter-site
    31	offset and the predicted geometric delay; a residual of zero indicates perfect
    32	agreement.
    33	
    34	\subsubsection{Chance-coincidence probability}
    35	\label{sec:toa-pcc}
    36	
    37	A small timing residual shows that a CHIME and a DSA trigger are \emph{consistent}
    38	with being the same burst; it does not by itself show that they \emph{are}. The
    39	complementary question is how often an \emph{unrelated} CHIME burst would fall
    40	close enough to a given DSA burst---in arrival time, sky position, and dispersion
    41	measure simultaneously---to mimic the association by chance. We quantify this with
    42	a chance-coincidence (false-alarm) probability $P_{\rm cc}$.
    43	
    44	We model unrelated CHIME bursts as a Poisson point process on the sky. For DSA
    45	burst $i$, the expected number of unrelated CHIME bursts that land inside the joint
    46	association window is
    47	\begin{equation}
    48	\mu_i = R \,\Omega_{\rm win}\, (2\,\Delta t)\, f_{\rm DM}(\mathrm{DM}_i,\Delta\mathrm{DM}),
    49	\label{eq:pcc_mu}
    50	\end{equation}
    51	where $R$ is the all-sky CHIME burst rate (per unit solid angle per unit time),
    52	$\Omega_{\rm win}$ is the sky area of the positional window, $2\,\Delta t$ is the
    53	full arrival-time window (a $\pm\Delta t$ coincidence gate), and
    54	$f_{\rm DM}(\mathrm{DM}_i,\Delta\mathrm{DM})$ is the fraction of the CHIME DM
    55	distribution lying within $\pm\Delta\mathrm{DM}$ of the burst's DM---the factor
    56	that additionally requires a chance interloper to match in dispersion, not only in
    57	time and position. For such a rare-event Poisson process the probability that
    58	\emph{at least one} unrelated burst falls in the window is
    59	\begin{equation}
    60	P_{{\rm cc},i} = 1 - e^{-\mu_i} \simeq \mu_i \qquad (\mu_i \ll 1),
    61	\label{eq:pcc_p}
    62	\end{equation}
    63	so $P_{\rm cc}$ is, to excellent approximation, just the expected number of chance
    64	matches.
    65	
    66	Equation~\eqref{eq:pcc_mu} conditions on a single DSA burst, so the sample-level
    67	false-alarm expectation is the sum $\sum_j \mu_j$ over the \emph{trial set}: the
    68	complete list of DSA-110 triggers searched for a CHIME/FRB counterpart over the
    69	2022~February--2024~February overlap, not merely the twelve that matched. The
    70	look-elsewhere penalty is therefore carried explicitly by that sum. Because each
    71	per-event expectation is only $\mu_j\sim5\times10^{-9}$ under the
    72	chance-maximizing windows below, the trial sum stays orders of magnitude below
    73	unity even when the entire DSA-110 detection list---of order $10^{2}$--$10^{3}$
    74	events across the two-year window---is taken as the denominator
    75	($\sum_j\mu_j\lesssim10^{-5}$). The value $\sum_i\mu_i$ reported below is summed
    76	over the twelve reported pairs; extending it to the full trial set multiplies it
    77	by at most the ratio of the searched-to-matched count and leaves the conclusion
    78	unchanged.
    79	
    80	We evaluate Eq.~\eqref{eq:pcc_mu} with deliberately conservative,
    81	chance-\emph{maximizing} inputs, so the reported $P_{\rm cc}$ is an upper bound on
    82	the true false-alarm rate: an all-sky CHIME rate rounded up to
    83	$R = 10^{3}\,\mathrm{sky^{-1}\,day^{-1}}$ (above the Catalog~1 value of
    84	$\simeq525\,\mathrm{sky^{-1}\,day^{-1}}$; \citealt{CHIME2021Catalog}), a
    85	$0.5^{\circ}$-radius positional disk ($\Omega_{\rm win}=0.785\,\mathrm{deg^2}$),
    86	$\Delta t = \pm1\,\mathrm{s}$, and $\Delta\mathrm{DM}=\pm5\,\mathrm{pc\,cm^{-3}}$.
    87	These windows are far wider than the actual per-event tolerances (millisecond
    88	timing, arcsecond-scale DSA localization, and the measured DM agreement), so using
    89	each event's true tolerances can only lower $P_{\rm cc}$. As an independent check,
    90	a direct Monte-Carlo of the background point process reproduces the closed-form
    91	$P_{\rm cc}$ to $0.3\%$ in a regime where both are measurable, and the result is
    92	stable across several orders of magnitude of variation in each window and in $R$.
    93	
    94	\subsubsection{Residuals and systematics}
    95	
    96	Table~\ref{tab:sample} summarizes the association diagnostics. The tabulated
    97	residuals use the shared DSA-DM convention: the CHIME and DSA arrivals are both
    98	referenced to 400\,MHz with the DSA catalog DM, so the residual is a
    99	timing/geometry consistency check rather than an independent per-telescope-DM
   100	comparison. Re-referencing the CHIME arrivals with their independently measured
   101	DMs would inject millisecond-scale shifts for the largest CHIME--DSA DM offsets,
   102	so those shifts are tracked separately rather than folded into the association
   103	residual.
   104	
   105	We adopt the sign convention $\Delta t = \Delta t_{\rm inter\text{-}site} -
   106	\tau_{\rm geo}$, where $\Delta t_{\rm inter\text{-}site}$ is the DSA-minus-CHIME
   107	arrival offset after both are referenced to 400\,MHz (Eq.~\ref{eq:dmdelay}) and
   108	$\tau_{\rm geo}$ is the predicted geometric delay (Eq.~\ref{eq:geodelay}); a
   109	positive residual means the DSA arrival is later than the shared-DM geometric
   110	prediction relative to CHIME, and $\Delta t=0$ is exact agreement. The geometric
   111	term is itself bounded: for the $1345\,\mathrm{km}$ CHIME (DRAO)--DSA-110 (OVRO)
   112	baseline, $|\tau_{\rm geo}| \le |\mathbf{p}_2-\mathbf{p}_1|/c \approx 4.5\,$ms for
   113	any source direction. The residual is therefore not error-free but carries a
   114	per-burst systematic of order the inter-site clock/timestamp alignment
   115	($\sigma_{\rm clock}\sim1$\,ms) plus, for the eight DM-constrained bursts, the
   116	residual dispersion left by the CHIME--DSA DM offset---up to $\sim\!12$\,ms of
   117	CHIME-referenced shift for the largest offset (FRB~20220207C, $\Delta\mathrm{DM}
   118	=-0.84\,\mathrm{pc\,cm^{-3}}$) were the CHIME arrival re-referenced at its own DM,
   119	a convention-dependent term reported separately rather than folded in. We accept
   120	a pair when its residual is consistent with the geometric delay to within this
   121	combined budget---order several ms, and up to $\sim\!10$\,ms where the CHIME--DSA
   122	DM offset is largest; all twelve residuals ($-2.7$ to $+8.4$\,ms) satisfy it,
   123	whereas a residual of several tens of ms, inconsistent with the
   124	$\lesssim\!4.5$\,ms geometric delay and the timing budget, would fail. We stress
   125	that the association verdict does not rest on the timing residual alone: the
   126	decisive gates are the chance-coincidence probability, positional coincidence,
   127	and (for the DM-constrained bursts) DM agreement, for which the residual is a
   128	supporting consistency diagnostic.
   129	
   130	Evaluated with the conservative windows above, all twelve bursts have
   131	per-burst chance-coincidence probabilities of order $10^{-9}$ and thus
   132	$P_{\rm cc}<10^{-8}$ individually; the expected number of chance associations
   133	across the full sample is $\sum_i\mu_i \approx 5.5\times10^{-8}$. In other words,
   134	even under windows chosen to overstate the false-alarm rate, fewer than one in a
   135	hundred million such pairings would be expected to arise by chance per pair
   136	(and the sample-summed expectation is about one in twenty million). Because the
   137	estimate is so far below any plausible threshold, the chance-coincidence test on
   138	its own establishes the co-detections; the positional-coincidence and
   139	DM-agreement checks only tighten an already-decisive result. All twelve pass the
   140	positional-coincidence check. The eight bursts with constrained CHIME-side DMs
   141	also pass the CHIME--DSA DM-agreement check; the four remaining bursts are marked
   142	`assoc.~(pos.)' in Table~\ref{tab:sample}: their association rests on
   143	position and timing alone, because their CHIME sub-band fits did not provide
   144	a constraining independent DM. For those four we set $f_{\rm DM}=1$ in
   145	Eq.~\eqref{eq:pcc_mu}---the dispersion criterion is not imposed, so their
   146	$P_{\rm cc}$ is a time-and-position bound only, consistent with the
   147	position-only verdict; the eight DM-constrained bursts carry the full
   148	$f_{\rm DM}(\mathrm{DM}_i,\Delta\mathrm{DM})<1$ factor. The reported
   149	$P_{\rm cc}$ values are thus computed per association class, and the
   150	position-only $P_{\rm cc}$ are, if anything, conservative relative to the
   151	DM-constrained ones.
   152	
   153	The per-burst DM provenance (per-telescope DM, method, and CHIME--DSA agreement)
   154	and the intermediate association diagnostics are provided as a machine-readable
   155	table in the archival data release (Section~\ref{sec:data-availability});
   156	Table~\ref{tab:sample} carries the manuscript-facing timing residual, $P_{\rm cc}$,
   157	and association-verdict columns. We note in passing that the eight
   158	DM-constrained bursts show CHIME reading systematically low relative to DSA by
   159	$\approx0.4\,\mathrm{pc\,cm^{-3}}$ on average---a coherent, sub-floor offset that
   160	does not affect any association verdict.
########## sections/budget.tex ##########
     1	% Core methods section. The observed DM decomposes as
     2	%   DM_obs = DM_MW,ISM + DM_MW,halo + <DM_cosmic>(z) + DM_intervening + DM_host,
     3	% with DM_MW,ISM from NE2001/YMW16 (pygedm), DM_MW,halo a 40 pc/cm^3 prior
     4	% (Yamasaki & Totani 2020), <DM_cosmic>(z) the Macquart relation
     5	% (Macquart et al. 2020; f_IGM=0.84, chi_e=0.875), and DM_intervening a two-phase
     6	% (hot mNFW + clumpy cool) CGM column for each intersecting foreground galaxy,
     7	% reported raw and capped at b = 0.1 R_vir (the galaxy-interior regime).
     8	%
     9	% Scattering: measured tau_1GHz from nested-sampling fits (quality-gated on
    10	% reduced chi^2, with R^2/normality informational only), compared against the
    11	% predicted intervening tau from a thin-screen two-phase model.
    12	
    13	\subsection{Dispersion Measure Decomposition}
    14	\label{sec:dm}
    15	
    16	The observed dispersion measure of each co-detection separates into Galactic,
    17	cosmological, host, and (where the sightline intersects a foreground galaxy)
    18	intervening contributions \citep{CordesChatterjee2019},
    19	\begin{equation}
    20	\mathrm{DM_{obs}} = \mathrm{DM_{MW}} + \langle\mathrm{DM_{cosmic}}\rangle(z)
    21	+ \mathrm{DM_{int}} + \mathrm{DM_{host}},
    22	\label{eq:dmbudget}
    23	\end{equation}
    24	with the Galactic term $\mathrm{DM_{MW}} = \mathrm{DM_{MW,ISM}} +
    25	\mathrm{DM_{MW,halo}}$ split between disk and halo. We estimate the disk
    26	contribution $\mathrm{DM_{MW,ISM}}$ from the NE2025 \citep{Ocker2026}
    27	electron-density model integrated to $30\,\mathrm{kpc}$ (consistent with the
    28	Galactic characterization of Section~\ref{sec:obs-fg}), with the older NE2001
    29	\citep{CordesLazio2002} and YMW16 \citep{YMW2017} models retained as
    30	cross-checks, and adopt a $\sim$40\,pc\,cm$^{-3}$ Milky-Way halo prior
    31	\citep{YamasakiTotani2020}. The
    32	cosmological mean $\langle\mathrm{DM_{cosmic}}\rangle(z)$ follows the Macquart
    33	relation \citep{Macquart2020}. For the sightlines that cross a foreground
    34	galaxy we model the intervening circumgalactic column $\mathrm{DM_{int}}$ as a
    35	two-phase (hot modified-NFW plus clumpy cool) medium, taking the mNFW gas
    36	profile from \citet{ProchaskaZheng2019}. Such intervening halos can imprint
    37	measurable dispersion and, where the gas is turbulent, scattering on FRB
    38	sightlines \citep{Ocker2021}, although an individual quiescent halo may instead
    39	yield only an upper limit on its scattering contribution \citep{Prochaska2019}.
    40	
    41	Rather than attribute the arithmetic residual of the mean-subtracted budget to
    42	the host, we forward-model $\mathrm{DM_{host}}$ as a posterior. The cosmological
    43	term is drawn from the full, skewed conditional distribution
    44	$P(\mathrm{DM_{cosmic}}\,|\,z)$---the \citet{Macquart2020} functional form with
    45	fractional scatter $\sigma_{\rm DM}=F\,z^{-1/2}$, $F\in[0.25,0.40]$
    46	\citep{McQuinn2014, James2022}---and the Galactic-disk, Galactic-halo, and
    47	intervening columns are drawn from their own priors
    48	(Appendix~\ref{app:host-forward-model}). We evaluate
    49	$\mathrm{DM_{host}}=\mathrm{DM_{obs}}-\mathrm{DM_{MW}}-\mathrm{DM_{cosmic}}-
    50	\mathrm{DM_{int}}$ per draw. Because $P(\mathrm{DM_{cosmic}})$ is right-skewed,
    51	subtracting its \emph{mean}---as an arithmetic residual does---biases the
    52	inferred host low; sampling the full distribution removes that bias and returns
    53	an asymmetric posterior with a well-defined $P(\mathrm{DM_{host}}<0)$ per
    54	sightline (Table~\ref{tab:budget}, Appendix~\ref{app:host-forward-model}).
    55	
    56	\subsection{Intervening Foreground Galaxies and Clusters}
    57	\label{sec:foreground}
    58	
    59	The intervening term $\mathrm{DM_{int}}$ is sourced only where a sightline
    60	actually crosses foreground circumgalactic or intracluster gas. We therefore
    61	build $\mathrm{DM_{int}}$ from the confirmed foreground systems of the census
    62	(Section~\ref{sec:obs-fg}, Table~\ref{tab:foreground}), and only from those
    63	whose impact parameter places the sightline inside the gas: for galaxies we
    64	integrate the column to the halo and cap the reported value at $b = 0.1\,R_{\rm
    65	vir}$, the galaxy-interior regime; for clusters we require the sightline to pass
    66	within $R_{500}$ ($b/R_{500}\le 1$), a conservative aperture that across the
    67	whole sample selects a single cluster (FRB~20230307A, J115120.4$+$714435 at
    68	$b/R_{500}=0.83$). Refuted and inconclusive systems, and confirmed systems whose
    69	sightline passes outside this aperture, do not contribute.
    70	
    71	For each retained system we model the gas as a two-phase medium---a hot
    72	modified-NFW component \citep{ProchaskaZheng2019} plus a clumpy cool
    73	component---and integrate the electron column along the impact-parameter chord.
    74	The cluster contribution uses the $M_{500}$ and $R_{500}$ of the
    75	\citet{WenHan2024} catalog to fix the halo, evaluating the intracluster
    76	dispersion on the modified-NFW profile at the sightline's impact parameter.
    77	The $b\le R_{500}$ cut retains just the one cluster that pierces the halo; the
    78	per-cluster dispersion columns for all four foreground clusters along the
    79	FRB~20230307A sightline are shown in Figure~\ref{fig:clusters_icm}
    80	(Appendix~\ref{app:clusters}).
    81	
    82	\subsection{Scattering Attribution}
    83	\label{sec:scattering}
    84	
    85	We measure the pulse-broadening time and its frequency scaling from the
    86	co-detected dynamic spectra and compare them against the scattering predicted
    87	by each candidate screen: the Milky-Way ISM, an intervening circumgalactic
    88	medium, or a host galaxy, where the host can dominate the scattering of a
    89	heavily broadened burst \citep{Ocker2022}. The fits are produced with a common
    90	open-source reduction and fitting pipeline.
    91	
    92	\subsubsection{The joint two-band fit}
    93	\label{sec:jointfit}
    94	\label{sec:beta-scattering-methods}
    95	
    96	A single-band scattering fit cannot separate the scattering timescale from its
    97	frequency scaling, because the pulse broadening
    98	$\tau(\nu)=\tau_{1\,\mathrm{GHz}}\,(\nu/1\,\mathrm{GHz})^{-\alpha}$ enters a
    99	single band only through its value at that band's frequency: one band fixes the
   100	product, not the slope. Co-detection breaks this degeneracy---the $\sim$1\,GHz
   101	lever arm between CHIME/FRB ($\sim$0.4--0.8\,GHz) and DSA-110
   102	($\sim$1.4\,GHz) measures the frequency scaling directly---but breaking the
   103	degeneracy is not the same as measuring $\alpha$ in a physically consistent way.
   104	
   105	The pulse-broadening function (PBF) shape and the scattering index $\alpha$ are
   106	not independent observables. Both descend from the same turbulence spectrum: a
   107	power-law electron-density fluctuation spectrum
   108	$P_n(q)\propto q^{-\beta}$ produces a phase structure function
   109	$D_\phi(\rho)\propto\rho^{\beta-2}$, whose Fourier transform determines the
   110	angular brightness distribution and hence the PBF shape, while the diffractive
   111	scale and Fresnel arguments give the frequency scaling
   112	$\tau\propto\nu^{-2\beta/(\beta-2)}$ for a thin screen---i.e.\
   113	$\alpha=2\beta/(\beta-2)$ \citep{Rickett1990, Cordes2025}. The common
   114	one-sided exponential PBF,
   115	$p_E(t,\tau)=\tau^{-1}e^{-t/\tau}\,\Theta(t)$, corresponds to a square-law
   116	structure function $D_\phi(\rho)\propto\rho^2$ ($\beta=4$), for which
   117	$\alpha=4$. A Kolmogorov spectrum ($\beta=11/3$) instead yields a
   118	power-law PBF tail $p_d(t)\propto t^{-\beta/2}$ and $\alpha\approx4.4$
   119	\citep{Cordes2025}.
   120	
   121	Because $\beta$ determines both the PBF shape and $\alpha$, they must be
   122	co-modeled. Choosing a fixed PBF family and freely varying $\alpha$ inside it
   123	is physically inconsistent: the exponential PBF assumes square-law
   124	statistics, which already imply $\alpha\approx4$. If the true medium is
   125	Kolmogorov---or anything other than square-law---fitting an exponential PBF
   126	will absorb the shape mismatch into a biased $\alpha$ \citep{Cordes2025}.
   127	
   128	We therefore replace the phenomenological pair $(\tau_{1\,\mathrm{GHz}},\alpha)$
   129	with the turbulence spectral index $\beta$ as the fundamental parameter. For
   130	each $\beta$ in the fit, the PBF shape is computed directly from the
   131	corresponding phase structure function $D_\phi(\rho)\propto\rho^{\beta-2}$, and
   132	the frequency scaling index follows as $\alpha=2\beta/(\beta-2)$ under the
   133	thin-screen assumption. The free parameters of the joint fit are
   134	$(\tau_{1\,\mathrm{GHz}},\beta)$ and the per-telescope pulse parameters
   135	(arrival time $t_0$, intrinsic width $\zeta$, and a residual dispersion
   136	$\delta\mathrm{DM}$). The fit operates on the CHIME/FRB and DSA-110 dynamic
   137	spectra simultaneously, with the PBF shape and $\alpha$ both derived from
   138	$\beta$ at every likelihood evaluation rather than chosen independently.
   139	
   140	Under this model, the Kolmogorov value $\beta=11/3$ ($\alpha\approx4.4$) and the
   141	square-law limit $\beta=4$ ($\alpha=4$, exponential PBF) are specific points in
   142	the physically allowed thin-screen inertial-range mapping. Because
   143	$\alpha=2\beta/(\beta-2)$ decreases as $\beta$ increases in this regime, a
   144	formally shallow empirical scaling is not interpreted by itself as a turbulence
   145	spectral index; it instead indicates that the assumed scattering family, burst
   146	morphology, or inertial-range closure is incomplete.
   147	
   148	The square-law endpoint requires a further caution that we build into the
   149	results rather than append as a caveat. When the diffractive scale drops below
   150	the turbulence inner scale, the phase structure function is locally quadratic
   151	and $\alpha\to4$ regardless of the underlying spectral index, so an
   152	inner-scale-dominated medium and a genuine $\beta=4$ spectrum are observationally
   153	degenerate \citep{Cordes2016, Cordes2025}. A posterior that concentrates at
   154	$\beta=4$ is therefore \emph{not} reported as a measurement of square-law
   155	turbulence: it marks the closure regime in which the thin-screen inertial-range
   156	mapping no longer determines $\beta$. Accordingly, the per-sightline results
   157	table (Section~\ref{sec:results-alpha}) will carry an explicit closure-regime
   158	label---interior (a resolved $\beta$ within the inertial range) versus
   159	square-law/inner-scale limited---alongside each entry, so that no square-law
   160	endpoint is read as an inertial-range spectral index.
   161	
   162	\subsubsection{Sub-band validation of the frequency scaling}
   163	\label{sec:subband}
   164	\label{sec:beta-subband}
   165	
   166	The frequency scaling recovered by the joint fit can be validated independently
   167	of the PBF parametrization by fitting the scattering time directly in frequency
   168	sub-bands. We split each band into sub-bands (four across CHIME, three across
   169	DSA-110), collapse each to a one-dimensional profile, and fit a scattered
   170	pulse---a Gaussian intrinsic shape convolved with a one-sided exponential tail,
   171	the exponentially-modified Gaussian---to each sub-band independently, leaving the
   172	scattering time $\tau$ free at every frequency. Because each $\tau_i$ is measured
   173	per sub-band with no power law imposed, the slope of $\tau_i(\nu_i)$ is an
   174	estimate of the frequency scaling that shares neither parametrization nor
   175	likelihood with the joint two-dimensional fit.
   176	
   177	This cross-check is a slope diagnostic, not an independent turbulence
   178	measurement, and we treat it as such for a specific reason. Each sub-band $\tau_i$
   179	is itself fit with an exponential (EMG) kernel---the $\beta=4$ family the joint
   180	fit is designed to avoid privileging---so each $\tau_i$ inherits a shape-mismatch
   181	bias wherever the true PBF is not exponential. The sub-band \emph{slope} is
   182	informative only insofar as that bias is common to all sub-bands and cancels in
   183	the ratio; it does not cancel when the mismatch is frequency-dependent, which is
   184	the generic case (the fraction of the pulse in the power-law tail, and hence the
   185	bias on a per-band exponential $\tau_i$, itself varies with frequency). We
   186	therefore report the sub-band slope as a validation diagnostic---a consistency
   187	check on the joint-fit frequency scaling---and never as a measurement of the
   188	turbulence spectral index. The sub-band validation figure and any event-level
   189	sub-band comparisons are deferred pending completion of the scattering
   190	analysis.
   191	
   192	% TODO(methods-subband-validation): Restore a sub-band validation artifact only
   193	% after fit re-validation. The restored figure should state the included bursts,
   194	% exclusion reasons, fit family, and whether the sub-band slopes are used as a
   195	% validation diagnostic or as physical turbulence constraints.
   196	
   197	\subsubsection{Gain-marginalized likelihood}
   198	\label{sec:gainmarg}
   199	\label{sec:beta-gainmarg}
   200	
   201	The per-channel amplitude of an FRB spectrum is unknown and strongly modulated
   202	by scintillation; carried as a free spectral envelope it leaves correlated
   203	structure in the fit residuals and invalidates a $\chi^2$ goodness-of-fit test.
   204	We therefore marginalize the per-channel gain analytically. With a Gaussian
   205	prior on the gain the per-channel evidence is available in closed form (a
   206	matched-filter likelihood), and the recovered gain absorbs both the burst
   207	spectrum and the scintillation pattern. This removes the spectral and
   208	scintillation structure from the residual, so that the reduced $\chi^2$ and the
   209	lag-1 residual autocorrelation become meaningful quality gates, and it lowers
   210	the sampled dimensionality.
   211	
   212	Because the marginalization deliberately absorbs the scintillation pattern into
   213	the gain, the scattering fit does not measure scintillation: the
   214	decorrelation-bandwidth products used later
   215	(Section~\ref{sec:results-scintillation}) come from a separate
   216	autocorrelation-function analysis path applied to the same dynamic spectra, not
   217	from this likelihood. The one interaction to guard against is at low
   218	signal-to-noise, where a fully free per-channel gain can absorb genuine
   219	scattering-induced spectral smearing into the spectral envelope and thereby bias
   220	the scattering time low; we therefore keep the gain prior informative (finite
   221	width) rather than flat, and verify on the final fits that the
   222	$\tau_{1\,\mathrm{GHz}}$ posterior is stable against the gain-prior width before
   223	quoting any low-$\tau$ result. We
   224	sample the posterior by nested sampling \citep{Skilling2004}, as implemented in
   225	\texttt{dynesty} \citep{Speagle2020}, and quality-gate each fit on its reduced
   226	$\chi^2$ and residual whiteness, treating $R^2$ and residual normality as
   227	informational.
   228	
   229	\subsubsection{Multiple temporal components}
   230	\label{sec:multicomp}
   231	\label{sec:beta-multicomp}
   232	
   233	Some bursts comprise more than one temporal sub-component, a common feature of
   234	the resolved FRB population \citep{Hessels2019, Pleunis2021}. An unmodeled
   235	second component is partially absorbed by a single scattered pulse---as a steeper
   236	frequency scaling and a larger scattering time---and so biases the inferred
   237	$\beta$, distorting both the fitted PBF shape and $\alpha$ simultaneously. We
   238	extend the likelihood to $N$ temporal components per band, each contributing its
   239	own $(t_0,\zeta)$ while $(\tau_{1\,\mathrm{GHz}},\beta)$ remain shared across
   240	both bands and all components, and we select the number of components per band
   241	by Bayesian evidence subject to a residual check. Because the evidence
   242	normalization differs between
   243	likelihood variants, the component counts are compared on a single matched grid
   244	in which even the one-component model is evaluated through the multi-component
   245	likelihood, so that evidence differences are meaningful. Component arrival times
   246	within a band are ordered and separated by a data-derived minimum spacing,
   247	keeping the fit identifiable at every prior sample.
   248	
   249	The evidence comparison is not by itself the final acceptance gate. For each
   250	candidate multiplicity we inspect the two-dimensional data, best-fit model, and
   251	whitened residual dynamic spectra in both bands. A component choice is rejected
   252	and re-fit if coherent burst power remains in the residuals, or if the collapsed
   253	profiles show a resolved sub-component that is absent from the model, even when
   254	the scalar goodness-of-fit statistics are otherwise acceptable. This visual and
   255	residual audit is part of the component-selection procedure: current rows with
   256	missed sub-components remain diagnostic only until their higher-multiplicity
   257	fits pass the same check. A clean single-component co-detection in our sample,
   258	for which the joint fit returns a well-constrained $\beta$ with white residuals,
   259	validates the method; accepted multi-component cases will be presented in
   260	Section~\ref{sec:results-alpha}.

value (raw core-extrapolated column $71\,\mathrm{pc\,cm^{-3}}$).}
\tablenotetext{u}{Position lies outside the deep-imaging survey footprints
(DESI Legacy DR8-North; SDSS DR12 returns no redshifts here), so only the
shallow all-sky catalogs (NED, GLADE+) constrain the sightline: the

\tablenotetext{m}{Confirmed foreground halos are present but lie beyond the
virial radii implied by their assumed halo masses

two-phase column vanishes. The fallback mass is a fiducial stellar mass
($\log M_\star/M_\odot\approx10$, the midpoint of the $9$--$11$ range spanned by
the sensitivity prior families) converted to halo mass through the
\citet{Moster2013} stellar-to-halo-mass relation. This is a median guess, not a
conservative lower bound, so its sign is not one-directional:
$\mathrm{DM_{int}}=0$ here is conditional on the assumed mass, and a more
massive (or a measured) halo would bring the sightline inside $R_{\mathrm{vir}}$
and make the column nonzero.}
\tablecomments{Because the cosmological term is drawn from a highly skewed
distribution, the host posteriors are broad and asymmetric and their medians
exceed the naive mean-subtracted residuals. The two highest-redshift sightlines
(FRB 20220310F, FRB 20221203A; $z=0.48$, $0.51$), where the Macquart term
dominates the budget, have host posteriors consistent with zero
($P(\mathrm{DM_{host}}<0)\approx0.45$): their formerly negative point residuals
reflect sightline-to-sightline scatter about the cosmological mean rather than an
unphysical host. Per-sightline $P(\mathrm{DM_{host}}<0)$ and the forward-model
priors are tabulated in Appendix~\ref{app:host-forward-model}.}

=====CONTEXT.md=====
# Faber2026 manuscript context

Overleaf-synced LaTeX for the CHIME/FRB–DSA-110 co-detection dispersion- and
scattering-budget paper. Numbers and figures are produced by **dsa110-FLITS**
(`pipeline/` submodule); fit-domain jargon lives in
[`pipeline/CONTEXT.md`](pipeline/CONTEXT.md).

## Language

**Co-detection sample**:
The twelve bursts seen by both CHIME/FRB and DSA-110 — the manuscript superset
for association, DM budget, and foreground census.
_Avoid_: treating any subset table as implicitly covering all twelve.

**Trust reset** (owner decisions 2026-07-06, evening→night, three waves —
supersedes every quoting carve-out below):
*Wave 1 (fits):* trust is revoked for ALL burst-data fits performed to date —
the joint scattering fits (every β, τ₁GHz, component multiplicity, and PPC
verdict; interior posteriors included, so freya β=3.72 and phineas β=3.23 are
NOT quotable), the sub-band EMG fits, the scintillation ACF fits (Δν_d), and
the spectral amplitudes c₀,γ with every derived energy.
*Wave 2 (census + budget):* trust is also revoked for the foreground census
(the 49-candidate catalog cross-match, its foreground/background/inconclusive
verdicts, impact parameters, and halo-mass proxies) and for the entire DM
budget decomposition (NE2001/YMW16 disk terms, the 40 pc cm⁻³ halo prior,
the Macquart mean, the mNFW/two-phase DM_int columns, host residuals, and
the negative-residual Macquart-scatter bound).
*Wave 3 (association + DM_obs):* trust is also revoked for the TOA
association arithmetic (residuals, P_cc, the association verdicts) and for
DM_obs across all twelve bursts. Grounds: the tabulated DM_obs does not
distinguish the CHIME-side from the DSA-side measurement, their level of
agreement has never been quantified, and how each DM_obs was obtained
(instrument pipeline, dedispersion method, reference artifact) is
undocumented.
**Status 2026-07-07:** V6 has revalidated this lane for manuscript use.
Association residuals, P_cc, and verdicts may be quoted from the pinned V6
artifacts under the shared DSA-DM reference convention; the residual is a
timing/geometry consistency check, not a per-telescope-DM-corrected residual.
DM_obs provenance and CHIME--DSA agreement are documented in the V6 artifacts.
**Status 2026-07-07 (V4+V5):** the owner has cleared the foreground-census (V4)
and DM-budget (V5) ladders on the strength of the DR9/DESI-DR1/NED/PS1-STRM
re-validation of the candidate catalog — including the DESI-STAR redshift
contaminant fix on the phineas cluster J114928.5+712526 and the discovery that
all census clusters come from the Wen \& Han 2024 DESI Legacy/WISE catalog (not
the X-ray/SZ catalogs the pinned code lists). The census now presents 35 systems
(34 halos + the single cluster within $R_{500}$; 14 further foreground clusters
at $b>R_{500}$ omitted). `tab:foreground`, the census verdicts/impact
parameters, the two-phase mNFW `DM_int` columns, and `fig:clusters_icm` are
restored for manuscript use. **Still revoked** (not unlocked by V4/V5): the
measured-scattering side of fig:budget and its measured-vs-predicted overlay
(wave-1 τ fits, V1 / plan D1), the τ·Δν_d two-screen test and scintillation
excess (pending the CHIME-band scint campaign), the FRB 20230913A intervening
attribution (rides on revoked scint strands), `tab:beta`, and
`tab:burst-energies`.
Outside lanes that have passed their V re-validation, no analysis product
retains trust. Raw observational inputs (positions, nicknames, published host
spectroscopic redshifts) remain inputs rather than revoked products; the V
ladders audit their provenance where consumed. The twelve-burst co-detection set
stays the *working roster*, with citable association evidence now restored by V6.
Manuscript claims consuming any revoked quantity are unsupported until
re-established — after the V4+V5 clearance above that still includes the
measured-scattering side of fig:budget and its measured-vs-predicted overlay
(wave-1 τ fits, V1 / plan D1), the τ·Δν_d two-screen test, the scintillation
excess, the FRB 20230913A intervening attribution (both of its supporting
diagnostics sit on revoked scint strands), `tab:beta`, `tab:burst-energies`,
and any association or DM_obs value not tied to the V6 artifacts and
shared-DSA-DM convention. Trust is restored only through the
re-validation ladders of `docs/rse/specs/plan-circulation-readiness.md` §V
(V1 fits, V3 energies, V4 census, V5 DM budget, V6 association + DM_obs).
_Avoid_: citing any fit-, census-, budget-, association-, or DM-derived
number from the current tables in new prose unless its lane has passed the
corresponding V ladder and the table/prose states the relevant convention;
treating a PPC pass under the old campaign as evidence of trust; quoting a
single undifferentiated DM_obs as if CHIME and DSA agree.

**Geometry-adjudicated β** (supersedes "rail-aware citable", owner decision
2026-07-06; quoting provisions suspended by the fit-trust reset above):
Rail classes from the β-coherent thin-screen campaign (interior / railed-hi /
railed-lo / unconstrained / gate-FAIL) are fit-campaign QA vocabulary and do
NOT appear in manuscript prose or tables. A posterior railing at β=4 is
evidence against the thin-screen closure for that sightline (ADR-0007's
re-open trigger — fired for ten of twelve posteriors: the nine railed
`tab:beta` rows plus gate-FAIL chromatica, the campaign report's 10-member
candidate set), not a square-law detection. The
former "geometry-conditioned α = 4 limit" is retired: **no α is quoted in any
form for an ex-railed row** until per-sightline geometry model selection
(thin screen vs extended medium, informed by the CHIME+DSA two-screen
scintillation constraints) adjudicates. What survives for those rows is the
descriptive data statement — PBF consistent with the exponential limit in both
bands (for the elevated-χ² trio, subject to pending (3)'s per-band systematics
caveat) — with no turbulence index attached; the statement is itself
fit-derived, so it too awaits §V re-validation before use. The two interior sightlines
(freya β=3.72, phineas β=3.23) remain quotable as thin-screen-conditioned
measurements with the geometry named, pending the same adjudication —
**suspended**: under the fit-trust reset the interior rows are not quotable
either until the fits are re-established. Rail-class tallies of the retired
fit campaign (2 interior / 9 railed / 1 gate-FAIL) remain quotable only as
facts about that campaign, never about the sky — these tallies are distinct
from the *foreground census*, which is wave-2 revoked; class fractions stay
withheld. Owner decision 2026-07-06: the tallies are **dropped from the
manuscript** — methods may describe the thin-screen campaign qualitatively
as model-family diagnosis, but no tally is quoted as a fact. The PBF shape and α = 2β/(β−2) are both derived
from β at each likelihood evaluation (co-model; sec:jointfit).
_Avoid_: α = 4 quoted for an ex-railed row in any form, including as a limit;
rail-class vocabulary in manuscript text beyond the campaign tallies;
thin-screen as an unstated default geometry; quoting median β or class
fractions.

**Wilhelm / EMG wording guardrail (2026-07-07):** For FRB 20221203A
(`wilhelm`), do not describe the residual morphology as evidence against the
right-sided exponential/EMG branch. The beta-coherent fits drive this sightline
to the β≈4 exponential limit. The remaining issue is a coherent high-S/N DSA
bright-pulse profile residual within that preferred exponential-tail model, not
a rejected EMG/PBF family.

**Scint→scattering coupling** (owner decision 2026-07-06; A1 mechanism
below adopted as a **working draft** 2026-07-06 late evening and re-opened
the same night — owner keeps all design decisions unlocked; treat every
element as revisable until the design discussion closes):
Scintillation is not a parallel product. The two-screen analysis (τ·Δν_d,
screen placement) built on the CHIME+DSA scintillation campaign — once it is
complete on both bands — feeds the per-sightline scattering geometry choice;
scattering re-fits and any restructured β presentation are sequenced behind
that completion. Mechanism (A1, working draft): a modular constraint layer — scint
products enter as frozen posterior/limit products with quality flags (never
point estimates); τ·Δν_d is used probabilistically to count screens and
derive τ_near/τ_dom; no second broadening component is fitted unless the
re-validated posterior makes it temporally resolvable (escalation trigger:
Pr(τ_near/τ_dom > 0.1) > 0.1, median ratio > 0.03, same-screen ambiguity,
or predicted-scale PPC residuals); for extended host media, quenching
constrains an effective source-proximate scattering-depth distribution, not
a point screen distance; scint geometry sets **prior odds** on the PBF
kernel family (thin vs extended) with final selection by
evidence/model-comparison; a joint burst-shape+ACF likelihood is deferred
unless modular products conflict on a high-S/N sightline. Full decision
text: plan-circulation-readiness A1.
_Avoid_: presenting scintillation and scattering as independent result
silos; treating a scint verdict as a hard geometry cut rather than prior
odds; fitting a second broadening component without the escalation trigger.

**Unified β roster** (suspended by the fit-trust reset — the row facts below
are history of the revoked campaign, not citable trust states):
One `tab:beta` listing every geometry-adjudicated-citable sightline (including
multiplicity cases such as FRB 20220310F), not a split table.
FRB 20220207C (zach) is **re-admitted** as a railed-hi Tier-B row — the
campaign's PPC-verified C1D1 joint fit (roster re-lock 2026-07-06, pipeline
PR #133) supersedes the Pass-2 fixed-s² C2D3-vs-C2D2 exclusion, which applied
to the retired free-α framework. FRB 20240203A (chromatica) is **excluded**
(joint-fit quality gate FAIL, χ²ᵣ ≈ 11.6/9.3).
_Avoid_: separate prose-only β for bursts that belong in the table.

**Energies trust boundary** (suspended by the fit-trust reset):
A row in `tab:burst-energies` requires spectroscopic host redshift and a joint
c₀,γ fit with physical per-band amplitudes. Scattering joint-fit FAIL is
informational (energy is β-independent). Pass 2 re-admits FRB 20220506D and
FRB 20220310F via mixed-legacy c₀,γ export (eight rows total). Under the
fit-trust reset no energy is currently citable; the fresh-read audit also
flagged a γ_D pile-up at ≈−5 (possible prior rail) and a selection-rule
contradiction (gate-FAIL 20240203A tabulated under a "quality-passing"
criterion) — both must be resolved in the re-validation pass.
_Avoid_: "six energies" or "FAIL-gated" exclusion for oran/whitney.

**Per-section sample rule**:
Every analysis subset states its own burst list and exclusion reasons in text or
caption; the twelve-burst co-detection set is never assumed as the denominator.

**Explicit pending**:
(0) Re-validation framework (plan §V) — the trust reset makes this the first
gate: no fit-, census-, budget-, association-, or DM-derived quantity is
citable until its producing analysis passes the corresponding re-trust
ladder (V1 fits, V3 energies, V4 census, V5 DM budget, V6 association +
DM_obs); includes verifying whether the scattering-fit CHIME
inputs share the gen-1 de-chirp defect lineage found in the scintillation
products. (1) Geometry-selection campaign (extended-medium kernel + per-sightline model
selection, scint-informed) — supersedes the narrower "ADR-0007 re-analysis of
the nine railed rows"; blocks any α quoting for the nine ex-railed rows and
the restructured abstract/co-model-methods/results language. (2) CHIME-band scintillation
campaign: burst configs + first measurements for whitney/phineas/mahi/isha;
U sizing + regeneration for the six never-generated co-detections; ACF/Δν_d
across the sample (the existing DSA-band Δν_d fits and two-screen table are
themselves revoked pending §V; the campaign re-establishes both bands).
(3) Per-band systematics pass on the sightlines the fresh campaign flags
with elevated per-band χ² (the revoked campaign's trio — wilhelm, hamilton,
zach — is the starting hypothesis, re-derived by plan C1). (4) Two-screen
treatment decision — **working draft adopted 2026-07-06** (modular
constraint layer with prior-odds geometry and a posterior escalation
trigger; see "Scint→scattering coupling" above), **design open**: owner
re-opened all locked decisions the same night; thresholds, trigger form,
and interface contract remain under discussion. (5) **Manuscript not yet
reconciled to this contract** — wave 1: abstract, observations (§2), the
co-model methods (sec:jointfit / sec:beta-scattering-methods), results,
discussion, conclusions, and `tab:beta` still carry rail-class language
and/or α = 4 limits, and the multiplicity-bias demonstration
(fig:whitney_mult, the abstract's closing claim, conclusions item 7) plus
fig:jointmodel_montage and fig:scint_screens are built on revoked fits;
wave 2: the budget section (§3 — census verdicts, dominant-systems and
cluster analyses, scint excess, τ·Δν_d test), results §4.1, and conclusions
items 1–3/5–6 carry census/budget claims now unsupported. Reconciliation is
scheduled per plan F1 (see `docs/rse/specs/plan-circulation-readiness.md`).
The measured-versus-predicted budget overlay exists in the draft, but both
sides are now revoked (measured diamonds = wave-1 τ fits; predicted bars =
wave-2 census/budget products) — re-derived after C + V4/V5 (plan D1).

**Pass 2 closeout (2026-06-27)**:
Pipeline PR #74 merged @ `c0696a6`; Faber2026 stacked PR pins submodule and
updates tex (8-row energies, zach out of `tab:alpha`, abstract/conclusions prose).

**β-model revision (2026-06-29) — campaign complete (2026-07-06)**:
Joint-fit methodology revised: the fundamental parameter is the turbulence
spectral index β (P_n(q) ∝ q^(−β)), from which both the PBF shape (via
D_φ(ρ) ∝ ρ^(β−2)) and the frequency-scaling index α = 2β/(β−2) follow. The
old free-α + fixed-exponential-PBF approach is physically inconsistent (Cordes
et al. 2025). `tab:alpha` → `tab:beta`; `fig:alpha_pbf` dropped. The
β-coherent thin-screen campaign (pipeline PRs #133/#134) re-fit all 12
co-detections with PPC verification: freya β=3.72 and phineas β=3.23
interior; 9 railed-hi table rows; chromatica gate-FAIL. Report:
`pipeline/analysis/beta_campaign/CAMPAIGN_REPORT.md`.

## Relationships

- **Geometry-adjudicated β** governs what `tab:beta` (**unified β roster**) may
  quote, while **explicit pending** governs population prose;
  **scint→scattering coupling** sequences the re-fit that will repopulate it.
- **Energies trust boundary** is independent of the β roster (different quality
  inputs: c₀,γ vs τ,β).
- **Per-section sample rule** applies to both tables and narrative subsets.

## Example dialogue

> **Dev:** "Nine rows rail at β=4 — can we say the sample's median α is 4?"
> **Domain expert:** "No — and under the fit-trust reset nothing from those
> fits is citable at all, not even the descriptive statement. The fits must
> first pass the plan-§V re-trust ladder on verified inputs; then geometry
> model selection, informed by re-validated two-screen scintillation
> constraints, decides what any surviving statement or index means. The
> rail tallies describe the retired fit campaign, not the sky."

## Flagged ambiguities

- **"Well-constrained"** in figures — after the fit-trust reset the term has
  no citable referent: nothing qualifies until a fit passes §V and geometry
  adjudication. Figures using it need relabeling during reconciliation.
  Profile-bias demonstrator claim remains withheld (ADR-0003).
- **Old α values** from the free-α + fixed-exponential-PBF model are retired;
  where they survive (the sub-band validation figure) they are labeled as
  exponential-parametrization cross-checks, not turbulence indices; under the
  fit-trust reset the sub-band fits themselves are also uncitable pending §V.
- **johndoeII's interior crossing** — its z=0.77 dominant halo is foreground
  only against a placeholder host redshift; the galaxy-interior attribution is
  provisional until a spectroscopic host z exists — and now doubly suspended:
  the census verdict itself is Wave-2 revoked pending V4.

candidate multiplicity we inspect the two-dimensional data, best-fit model, and
whitened residual dynamic spectra in both bands. A component choice is rejected
and re-fit if coherent burst power remains in the residuals, or if the collapsed
profiles show a resolved sub-component that is absent from the model, even when

residual audit is part of the component-selection procedure: current rows with
missed sub-components remain diagnostic only until their higher-multiplicity

cd /Users/jakobfaber/Developer/repos/github.com/jakobtfaber/Faber2026 && cat > /tmp/language_audit.md <<'EOF'
# Faber2026 — language / field-register audit

Scan for text that does not align with standard ApJ / FRB-field prose.
Locations are file:line. Comment-only (`%`) content noted separately.

## 1. Draft-scaffolding leaking into rendered prose (highest priority)
- main.tex:46-60 — five un-commented `[SLOT: ...]` placeholders inside the abstract; these TYPESET into the PDF.
- observations.tex:41 ("citable"), results.tex:153 ("restored as citable measurements") — "citable" is internal trust-ladder vocabulary.
- "deferred pending completion of the scattering analysis" (verbatim): observations.tex:43, budget.tex:189, results.tex:71, appendix.tex:145, methods.tex:56.
- "current" as draft-state flag: results.tex:152-154 (also "already informative", "remain diagnostic only until"), budget.tex:255.
- methods.tex:14 — "a trusted per-band amplitude fit" ("trusted" = trust-reset vocabulary).
- Section bodies are largely `% TODO(...)` comment blocks (discussion.tex nearly all TODO; also results/conclusions/budget/observations). Non-rendering, but Discussion & Conclusions are effectively unwritten.

## 2. Terminology inconsistencies (one thing, multiple names)
- Photo-z classifier named 3 ways: "WISE--PanSTARRS--STRM" (observations.tex:108), "PanSTARRS-STRM" (observations.tex:139), "PS1-STRM" (foreground_table.tex, throughout). Published name = PS1-STRM (Beck+2021).
- Survey release: prose "DESI Legacy ... DR9" (observations.tex:126,160,163) vs budget_table.tex:57 note u "DESI Legacy DR8-North". DR8 vs DR9.
- Zhou2021 estimator described as "deep-learning estimator" (observations.tex:106) AND "random-forest estimator" (observations.tex:127) for the same catalog — contradictory (it is random-forest).
- Cluster catalogs: PSZ2/MCXC/MCXC-II X-ray/SZ machinery described (observations.tex) but all clusters actually from Wen & Han 2024 (tables, CONTEXT.md). X-ray/SZ text sources nothing.

## 3. Coined / non-standard terms used as if standard
- "chance-maximizing windows": toa.tex:72, results.tex:15, conclusions.tex:12. (Field: "conservative"/"worst-case".)
- "co-model / co-models" (verb): main.tex:41, observations.tex:57, budget.tex:122. (Field: "jointly model".)
- "sub-floor offset": toa.tex:159. (Means "below the uncertainty floor".)
- "soft disk": observations.tex:183 — plotting-marker language in a physics caption.

## 4. Informal register in captions / appendix
- "at-a-glance cross-check", "qualitative sanity check": appendix.tex:5,16.
- "backlights": intro.tex:5 — borderline; does appear in FRB literature.

## Checked and OK
- Counts consistent: 15 confirmed + 13 inconclusive = 28 rows; 35 = 28 + 7 refuted; 8 "DM+pos." + 4 "pos." = 12; tau_int "<= 0.024 ms" = table max; "eleven" panels = 12 - chromatica (gate-FAIL).
- Uniform American spelling (-ize, "modeling"); no British/American mix.
- No burst-nickname leaks in rendered prose (only in figure filenames/labels).
EOF
cp /tmp/language_audit.md ./language_audit.md && echo saved
stdout: saved

stdout:
=== reproducibility-foreground-galaxies.md (discovery vs validation) ===

The live search is implemented by `galaxies.foreground.search.run_search`. It
runs all 12 configured sightlines from `galaxies.foreground.config.TARGETS`.

Catalog/query engines:

- NED cone search through `NedTapEngine`.
- VizieR GLADE+ (`VII/291/gladep`), DESI Legacy DR8 North photo-z
  (`VII/292/north`), and SDSS DR12 (`V/147/sdss12`).
- All-sky cluster catalogs through `ClusterEngine`: PSZ2, MCXC, MCXC-II.
- Optional DESI DR1 TAP search exists but is disabled by default

| Survey | Engine | Nominal footprint (this sample) |
|--------|--------|-----------------------------------|
| NED | `NedTapEngine` | all-sky |
| GLADE+ | `VizierEngine(VII/291/gladep)` | all-sky |
| DESI DR8 North | `VizierEngine(VII/292/north)` | Dec ≥ −20° |
| SDSS DR12 | `VizierEngine(V/147/sdss12)` | SDSS NGC (Dec ≥ 1.26°) |
| Clusters | `ClusterEngine` (PSZ2, MCXC, MCXC-II) | all-sky |

Status per cell: `no_footprint` · `footprint_empty` (in footprint, 0 cone hits) ·
`catalog_hits` · `foreground`.

- `normalize_codetection.py` parses the foreground spreadsheet exports into
  `bursts.csv` and `foreground.csv`, recomputing separations, impact parameters,
  and internal flags.
- `validate_foreground.py` independently queries LS DR9 photo-z, DESI DR1
  spec-z, NED, and SIMBAD around each spreadsheet object and writes
  `foreground_validated.csv`.
- `ps1_strm_resolve.py` / `ps1_strm_adjudicate.py` resolve the nine WISE/PS1/STRM
  halo rows whose spreadsheet redshifts were not independently sufficient.
- `merge_final.py` merges validation + STRM adjudication into
  `foreground_final.csv` and asserts 49 rows: 29 confirmed, 7 refuted,
=== validate_foreground.py header/sources ===

=== obs discovery paragraph 100-118 ===
search in two stages---discovery followed by external-catalog
validation---and record the result in Table~\ref{tab:foreground}.

The discovery stage searches a wide cone around each localized burst. At the
sample's high declination ($+70^\circ$ to $+74^\circ$) only all-sky or northern
catalogs provide coverage. For galaxy-scale halos we query the DESI Legacy
Imaging Surveys photometric-redshift catalog \citep[the random-forest estimator
of][]{Zhou2021}, GLADE+, and SDSS~DR12, supplemented by the
PS1-STRM photometric classifier \citep{Beck2021} for sources
without a Legacy match; for clusters we query the DESI Legacy/WISE cluster
catalog of \citet{WenHan2024}---which supplies $M_{500}$, $R_{500}$, and,
through its DESI members, a spectroscopic redshift---together with the all-sky
Planck Sunyaev--Zel'dovich (PSZ2) and X-ray (MCXC, MCXC-II) catalogs; at the
sample's declination every matched cluster comes from the optical catalog.
The search apertures set the census completeness and are fixed in physical
units. Galaxy cones extend to a fixed proper impact parameter at the
circumgalactic scale, and the recovered foreground galaxies span
$b\approx60$--$280\,\mathrm{kpc}$; cluster cones are far wider
($\sim\!5\,\mathrm{Mpc}$), and a cluster is retained when the sightline passes
=== cluster ClusterEngine sources (confirm PSZ2/MCXC queried; WenHan not in discovery) ===

-- Survey release: prose "DESI Legacy ... DR9" (observations.tex:126,160,163) vs budget_table.tex:57 note u "DESI Legacy DR8-North". DR8 vs DR9.
-- Zhou2021 estimator described as "deep-learning estimator" (observations.tex:106) AND "random-forest estimator" (observations.tex:127) for the same catalog — contradictory (it is random-forest).
-- Cluster catalogs: PSZ2/MCXC/MCXC-II X-ray/SZ machinery described (observations.tex) but all clusters actually from Wen & Han 2024 (tables, CONTEXT.md). X-ray/SZ text sources nothing.
+- Survey release: RESOLVED (not a conflict). Discovery stage uses DESI Legacy DR8 North (VizieR VII/292/north); validation independently queries DR9 photo-z. Two deliberate pipeline stages. Fix applied: discovery paragraph (observations.tex:105) now names "DR8 North" explicitly so it no longer reads as disagreeing with budget_table note u. DR9 in validation prose (126,160,163) is correct.
+- Zhou2021 estimator: FIXED. Was "deep-learning" (observations.tex:106) vs "random-forest" (127); it is random-forest — corrected to random-forest.
+- Cluster catalogs: RETRACTED (over-read, no change needed). Discovery ClusterEngine genuinely queries PSZ2/MCXC/MCXC-II (config.py:81-83); "every matched cluster comes from the optical catalog" correctly reports that only Wen & Han 2024 systems survived at this declination (V4-cleared, CONTEXT.md). Reporting searched space + yield is correct practice.

import subprocess, os
os.chdir("/Users/jakobfaber/Developer/repos/github.com/jakobtfaber/Faber2026")
def g(args, inp=None):
    return subprocess.run(["git"]+args, input=inp, capture_output=True, text=True)

# Pull origin/main blobs
main_tex = g(["show","origin/main:main.tex"]).stdout
obs_tex  = g(["show","origin/main:sections/observations.tex"]).stdout

# --- Transform main.tex: comment out any line starting with [SLOT ---
out=[]
slot=0
for ln in main_tex.split("\n"):
    if ln.startswith("[SLOT") :
        out.append("% "+ln); slot+=1
    elif slot and ln.startswith("$") and out and out[-1].startswith("% [SLOT"):
        # continuation line of a multi-line SLOT (starts with $... or text, next of a % SLOT)
        out.append("% "+ln)
    else:
        out.append(ln)
# handle multi-line SLOTs: any line that is a continuation (previous commented SLOT, current not blank, not new command)
# Redo more carefully:
lines = main_tex.split("\n")
out=[]; i=0; commented=0; incont=False
for ln in lines:
    if ln.startswith("[SLOT"):
        out.append("% "+ln); commented+=1; incont=not ln.rstrip().endswith("]")
    elif incont:
        out.append("% "+ln); incont=not ln.rstrip().endswith("]")
    else:
        out.append(ln)
new_main="\n".join(out)
print("SLOT lines commented:", commented)
print("remaining uncommented SLOT:", sum(1 for l in new_main.split(chr(10)) if l.startswith("[SLOT")))

# --- Transform observations.tex ---
o = obs_tex
assert "deep-learning estimator" in o
o = o.replace("the deep-learning estimator\nof", "the random-forest\nestimator of").replace("the deep-learning estimator of","the random-forest estimator of")
# DR8 label + PS1-STRM in discovery
o = o.replace("Imaging Surveys photometric-redshift catalog","Imaging Surveys DR8 North photometric-redshift catalog")
o = o.replace("WISE--PanSTARRS--STRM photometric classifier","PS1-STRM photometric classifier")
o = o.replace("PanSTARRS-STRM ``unsure''","PS1-STRM ``unsure''")
print("obs checks -> deep-learning left:", "deep-learning" in o,
      "| WISE--PanSTARRS left:", "WISE--PanSTARRS" in o,
      "| PanSTARRS-STRM left:", "PanSTARRS-STRM" in o,
      "| DR8 North present:", "DR8 North photometric" in o,
      "| PS1-STRM count:", o.count("PS1-STRM"))

# write blobs
def hashobj(content):
    r=g(["hash-object","-w","--stdin"], inp=content); assert r.returncode==0, r.stderr; return r.stdout.strip()
blob_main=hashobj(new_main)
blob_obs =hashobj(o)
print("blob_main",blob_main[:12],"blob_obs",blob_obs[:12])
open("/tmp/blobs.txt","w").write(f"{blob_main} {blob_obs}")
